title: Caucalis scabra
intro: Caucalis scabra, also known as Rough Wild Carrot, is a perennial plant species belonging to the family Apiaceae, characterized by its hairy stems and umbrella-like clusters of small white flowers. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health issues, including fever, respiratory problems, and gastrointestinal disorders, due to its anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and expectorant properties. The bioactive constituents of Caucalis scabra include volatile oils, flavonoids, and alkaloids, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. In traditional herbal medicine, the plant is often prepared as a tea, infusion, or tincture, and is also used in the form of dried roots and leaves.
taxonomy: Caucalis scabra is a species of flowering plant in the family Apiaceae, commonly known as the carrot or parsley family. It belongs to the genus Caucalis, which contains a small number of species found primarily in Europe. Caucalis scabra is native to the Mediterranean region and is often found in dry, sandy areas and rocky outcrops. The plant is a perennial with a slender stem and small, white or yellowish flowers that are arranged in umbels. In terms of classification, Caucalis scabra is a member of the kingdom Plantae, and its full binomial nomenclature is Caucalis scabra (L.) Sm.
